Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a proper study routine. I'd kind of drift through the week and then realize I had stuff due. Sometimes I'd have a burst of energy and study for hours, listening to lectures while doing other things, but other weeks I'd barely touch anything. I know I should be more organized, but I just can't seem to make myself care enough to stick to a schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without looking at course material, then cram when something was due. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. If there wasn't something urgent, I'd find literally anything else to do instead of studying.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an email reminder about something I'd forgotten. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I was missing, but I wouldn't actually do much once I was there. Occasionally a friend would mention something from class and I'd realize I was behind, so I'd log in to catch up.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. It never felt like a habit. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I was logging in more regularly because I was trying to keep up with discussion posts, but that faded pretty quickly. The LMS just felt like another thing I should be doing but wasn't.
Q5: The video lectures were actually helpful for me because I learn better by listening. I'd play them while doing other stuff, and somehow the information would stick. The discussion forums were okay too because seeing what other people were confused about helped me figure out what I needed to focus on.
Q6: I barely touched the readings and a lot of the supplementary materials. They felt like too much effort when I could just listen to the lectures instead. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came. The extra practice quizzes I mostly ignored because they weren't graded.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to the exam or if it was something I could listen to rather than read. Activities that felt like busy work just made me want to avoid them more. When something actually helped me understand a concept I was struggling with, that felt worthwhile.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, I just wouldn't do it. I know that's probably not great, but I already struggled to do the required stuff. Required activities at least had some pressure attached, even if I still left them until the last minute.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download the lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while walking around or doing chores. I also had some friends who would explain things to me, which helped way more than reading. A lot of my actual learning happened through conversations, not through the LMS.
Q10: There were times when I'd spend hours talking through concepts with classmates or listening to downloaded lectures, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also sometimes just think through problems in my head without writing anything down. The LMS only sees clicks, not actual understanding.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once and then replay it multiple times offline. Same with some of the slides – I'd save them to my phone and review them during random moments throughout the day. The LMS would just show one download, but I was actually engaging with that material repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely weeks where everything felt pointless and I just couldn't make myself care. High stress from other stuff in my life made it hard to focus on coursework. I'd open the LMS, stare at it, and then close it without doing anything. My motivation just wasn't there.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or tired, the LMS was the first thing I'd ignore. I had other responsibilities that felt more pressing, and coursework always seemed like something I could push to later. Confusion also made me avoid things – if I didn't understand something, I'd just not engage with it rather than struggle through.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but I'd be on my phone or thinking about something else entirely. Or I'd scroll through materials without actually absorbing anything. My body was present but my brain was somewhere else. It's like going through the motions without actually learning.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important but I'd just... not do it. I think it's partly because I felt overwhelmed and partly because I just didn't feel motivated. The more I avoided something, the harder it became to start. It's a terrible cycle and I know it, but knowing doesn't make it easier to stop.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me do anything. I'd ignore the LMS for days and then frantically log in the night before something was due. It's not a healthy pattern, but the pressure of a deadline was the only thing that could cut through my lack of motivation.
Q17: Feedback was kind of hit or miss for me. Sometimes getting a good grade would give me a temporary boost, but it wouldn't last. Bad feedback just made me feel worse and less likely to engage. The peer support stuff helped a bit because it felt less judgmental than official feedback.
Q18: The structure was okay, but having so many modules made it feel overwhelming. I'd see all these things I was supposed to do and just shut down. A simpler structure with fewer but more focused activities might have helped me stay on track better. The flexibility was nice but also meant I could keep putting things off.
Q19: The video-based content made engagement easier for me since I learn by listening. But having so many separate resources and activities made it harder – I'd lose track of what I'd done and what I still needed to do. Clearer priorities would have helped me focus on what actually mattered.
Q20: I think sometimes you're trying to figure out what you need to do without actually doing it. Or you're anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. For me, I might log in multiple times just to look at something without actually completing it because I couldn't make myself start.
Q21: That's basically me. I did well on exams because I'm actually pretty good at understanding things when I listen to them, even if I don't do all the coursework. A lot of my learning happened outside the LMS through downloaded materials and talking with friends. The system doesn't see that.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you were technically there, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with peers, the thinking you do away from the screen. They're just a partial picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's when you're curious and thinking about the material even when you're not actively studying. For me, engagement comes in bursts rather than being constant.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned from my peers or from listening to downloaded lectures repeatedly. It wouldn't show that despite my low motivation and sporadic attendance, I actually understood the material pretty well. It also wouldn't capture the stress and mental barriers that made it hard to engage consistently.
